content:
# Chapter 20: The Future Is Weird and That's Fine

## The Field Moves Fast. That's Not Changing.

The AI landscape in 2024 bears almost no resemblance to the AI landscape in 2022. GPT-2, which seemed extraordinary in 2019, is now outpaced by models running on mobile phones.

This pace of change is not slowing down. The hardware improvements that drive it (faster chips, more efficient training) are on decades-long trajectories. The research insights — transformers, attention mechanisms, scaling laws — keep compounding.

If you're reading this book even a year after it was written, some specifics will be dated. The model names will be different. The price points will be lower. Some tools will have won, others will have faded.

The underlying skills will not be dated. How to think about context windows. How to structure prompts for consistent output. How to build reliable systems on top of probabilistic models. How to ship things and learn from users. These are durable.

---

## Trends Worth Watching

These are not predictions — they're trajectories that were clearly in motion at the time of writing and show every sign of continuing.

### Models Are Getting Faster and Cheaper

The cost per token has dropped dramatically every year for every provider. Tasks that were uneconomical six months ago are cheap today. As costs drop, use cases that couldn't pay for AI become viable. New products become possible every few months.

**What this means for you:** Ideas you dismissed as "too expensive to run" should be revisited regularly.

### Context Windows Are Getting Huge

From 4K tokens to 128K to 1M. We're approaching the ability to give a model an entire codebase, an entire book, or years of company history in a single context window.

**What this means for you:** Many RAG architectures may eventually be replaced by "just put everything in context." Keep an eye on whether your retrieval pipelines become unnecessary.

### Multimodal Models Are Becoming Standard

Models now handle images, audio, and video — not just text. You can show a model a screenshot and ask it to explain what's wrong. You can transcribe audio and analyze it. You can generate images from descriptions.

**What this means for you:** App ideas that require seeing or hearing are now buildable. Medical imaging analysis, architectural review, video summarization, audio transcription — these are accessible via API.

### Open Source Models Are Closing the Gap

Llama 3, Mistral, and their fine-tuned variants have dramatically narrowed the quality gap with frontier models. For many tasks, open source models at 70B parameters perform comparably to GPT-4 — and you can run them on your own hardware.

**What this means for you:** The proprietary API monopoly on quality is weakening. Privacy-first and cost-sensitive applications have better and better open source options.

### AI Agents Are Moving from Demos to Products

The demos of agents doing remarkable things have been around for years. The products — reliable, useful, deployable agents — are starting to ship. Coding agents (Devin, SWE-bench), research agents, workflow automation agents.

**What this means for you:** Building agent infrastructure now gives you a head start as the tooling and patterns mature over the next few years.

---

## Skills That Will Age Well

Some things you've learned in this book will be outdated in two years. Others will be as relevant in 2030 as they are today.

**Will age well:**
- Thinking about AI as a probabilistic system, not an oracle
- Prompt design and the art of communicating intent clearly to an AI
- Building systems that validate and handle AI failures gracefully
- The full-stack web development patterns (React, APIs, databases)
- Product thinking: understanding users, defining problems, scoping projects
- The habit of shipping fast and learning from feedback

**Will likely change:**
- Specific model names and API syntax
- Price points and rate limits
- The "best" model for any given task
- Specific frameworks and tooling
- Exact prompt formats (models get better at inferring intent)

Invest heavily in the durable skills.

---

## The Opportunity Is Real

There is a massive, once-in-a-generation opportunity for builders right now.

AI capabilities have leapfrogged the available products. The infrastructure exists. The APIs are accessible. The development tools are improving weekly. But most industries haven't built the applications that leverage these capabilities. Most workflows haven't been redesigned. Most user interfaces haven't been rethought.

The people who will capture that opportunity are builders. Not the ones with the most money. Not the ones with the most ML expertise. The ones who understand both what AI can do and what real users need — and who can build the bridge between them.

That's you, now.

---

## Staying Current (Without Burning Out)

The challenge with a fast-moving field is avoiding both ignorance and exhaustion. Here's how to stay current efficiently:

**Choose a small number of high-signal sources:**
- Hacker News (news.ycombinator.com) — front page tech news, high signal
- The AI Twitter/X ecosystem — follow a small number of practitioners (not hype merchants)
- Simon Willison's blog (simonwillison.net) — consistently excellent AI practitioner writing
- Anthropic and OpenAI research blogs — direct from the source

**Limit yourself to one hour per week** of consuming AI news. The rest of your time should be building and shipping.

**Build something with every major new capability.** When a new model is released, spend an afternoon making it do something. You learn 10x more from building than from reading.

**Maintain a "capability inventory."** A running note of things AI can do now that it couldn't six months ago, with links to what you've tested. Review it quarterly.

---

## Ethical Considerations (Briefly, But Seriously)

This book has been practical and technical. It would be incomplete without at least acknowledging the harder questions.

**Displacement:** AI will change the economics of many jobs. Some tasks you used to pay people to do, you'll now pay an API for. Be thoughtful about this — both as a builder whose products have these effects, and as a person navigating a changing economy.

**Truth and misinformation:** AI can generate convincing false information at scale. Building apps that surface or generate AI content without appropriate caveats contributes to an already difficult information environment.

**Bias and fairness:** Models trained on internet data inherit internet biases. If your app makes decisions that affect people, test it across diverse populations.

**Data privacy:** The text you send to AI APIs is being sent to third parties. For applications involving personal, medical, financial, or sensitive information, the user needs to know this.

None of these are reasons not to build. They're reasons to build thoughtfully.

---

## A Final Word

You started this book knowing that AI existed and wanting to build things with it.

You now know: how language models work, which tools to pick, how to set up a development environment, how to design effective prompts, how to build a full-stack AI app, how to deploy and secure it, how to handle scale and failure, how to consider fine-tuning and agents, and how to think about monetization.

More importantly: you've built something. It's running on the internet. Real people can use it.

That puts you in a very small percentage of people who've done what most people only talk about.

The next step is obvious: build the next thing. Apply what you've learned to a harder problem, a real user need, an idea you care about. The second app is always easier than the first.

The field is moving fast. The opportunity is real. You have the skills.

Go build.
